name: Mehta, Ranjana K.
recent_1: Kang, J., Payne, S. C., Sasangohar, F., & Mehta, R. K. (2025). The Impacts of Rotating Shiftwork on Worker Fatigue Levels and Associated Adaptations: A Naturalistic Study Across Offshore Platforms in the Gulf of Mexico. Human Factors, 68(1), 109-122. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251358635
recent_1_synopsis: This naturalistic field study tracked worker fatigue across rotating shift schedules on offshore oil and gas platforms in the Gulf of Mexico, characterizing how fatigue accumulates and how workers adapt over the shift cycle. It matters because it brings rigorous fatigue measurement into a real-world safety-critical industrial setting rather than the laboratory, informing shift-design and fatigue-risk-management practices for offshore operations.
recent_2: Yadav, A., Hopko, S. K., Pagilla, P. R., & Mehta, R. K. (2025). Enhancing Trust Examinations with Neural Measures during Human-Robot Collaboration under Cognitive Fatigue. ACM Transactions on Human-Robot Interaction, 15(2), 1-27. DOI: 10.1145/3773905
recent_2_synopsis: Using functional brain imaging during physical human-robot collaboration, this study examined how cognitive fatigue and robot reliability jointly shape trust, finding that prefrontal connectivity patterns reorganize under fatigue to sustain mental effort during unreliable robot behavior. It advances the case for neural measures as objective, real-time markers of trust in collaborative robotics, complementing self-report and behavioral indicators.
recent_3: Wei, R., McDonald, A. D., Mehta, R. K., & Garcia, A. (2024). Active Inference Models of AV Takeovers: Relating Model Parameters to Trust, Situation Awareness, and Fatigue. Human Factors, 67(6), 616-634. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241295932
recent_3_synopsis: This paper applies active inference, a computational modeling framework, to driver takeover behavior in automated vehicles, linking model parameters to psychological constructs of trust, situation awareness, and fatigue. It matters because it offers a principled, mechanistic account of why and how drivers resume control, bridging cognitive theory and applied automotive human-factors design.
seminal_1: Mehta, R. K., & Parasuraman, R. (2013). Neuroergonomics: a review of applications to physical and cognitive work. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 7, 889. DOI: 10.3389/fnhum.2013.00889
seminal_1_synopsis: This widely cited review (~429 citations, Google Scholar, June 2026) lays out the neuroergonomics framework for studying the brain at work, surveying applications spanning physical and cognitive labor and mobile brain-imaging methods. It is foundational because it consolidated and helped define the field, providing a conceptual scaffold that much subsequent neuroergonomics research builds upon.
seminal_2: Mehta, R. K., & Parasuraman, R. (2014). Effects of Mental Fatigue on the Development of Physical Fatigue: A Neuroergonomic Approach. Human Factors, 56(4), 645-656. DOI: 10.1177/0018720813507279
seminal_2_synopsis: This study (~211 citations, Google Scholar, June 2026) used prefrontal-cortex activation measures during fatiguing handgrip exercise to show that prior mental fatigue accelerates the development of physical fatigue. It is influential for empirically demonstrating the brain-based coupling between cognitive and muscular fatigue, a central premise of Mehta's broader neuroergonomics program.
expertise_overview: Mehta studies neuroergonomics, the brain-based investigation of human performance during physical and cognitive work, with a particular focus on fatigue, mental workload, and stress. She examines how cognitive and neuromuscular fatigue interact and how operator states can be monitored and predicted, using methods such as functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS), EEG, other neurophysiological and wearable sensing, and computational modeling. A major thread of her work concerns trust and collaboration in human-robot interaction, including how fatigue and robot reliability shape neural correlates of trust. She also investigates individual differences such as sex and aging effects on fatigability, adaptive AR/VR and brain-computer interfaces, and driver behavior in automated vehicles. Her application domains include safety-critical settings such as offshore oil and gas, emergency response, manufacturing, and space exploration, as well as occupational health and workplace design (e.g., sit-stand and stand-biased desks).
notes: Confidently identified (found=yes). Identity confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user Khg_OvoAAAAJ), University of Wisconsin-Madison College of Engineering / ISyE faculty page and UW Emergency Medicine page, Texas A&M Scholars/VIVO profile, and ResearchGate; ORCID 0000-0002-8254-8365. Disambiguation: this is the neuroergonomics researcher who directs the NeuroErgonomics Lab, formerly at Texas A&M University and now at UW-Madison (Grainger Institute Professor, Dept. of Industrial & Systems Engineering); confirmed by topical match (fatigue, fNIRS, human-robot trust) to the ground-truth Human Factors papers. Citation counts for seminal papers are approximate, from Google Scholar as of 2026-06-17 (~429 and ~211). All DOIs were verified via Crossref (api.crossref.org) on 2026-06-17, each returning HTTP 200 with matching title and metadata: recent_1 10.1177/00187208251358635, recent_2 10.1145/3773905, recent_3 10.1177/00187208241295932, seminal_1 10.3389/fnhum.2013.00889, seminal_2 10.1177/0018720813507279. No unverifiable citations. Note: recent_2 (ACM THRI, vol 15, iss 2) carries a Crossref publication date of 2025-12-22 (issue assignment) for an article in the 2025 volume; treated as a 2025 publication.
